1: The guide takes Ezekiel northward into the outer court to the chamber complex opposite the separated yard — hammenuḥah and the building. The spatial movement in the vision narrative is always purposeful: each new zone is accessible only after the prior zone is fully measured, enacting the principle that entry into holiness requires preparation and sequence.
2: The north-facing structure — a hundred cubits long and fifty cubits wide. The explicit measurement of the chamber complex to the north of the sanctuary follows the same convention as the measurements of the sanctuary itself. The north/south pairing of priestly chamber complexes (v11 describes the south equivalent) creates the bilateral symmetry of the priestly precinct.
3: neged haʿesrim — opposite the twenty cubits of the inner court and opposite the pavement of the outer court: galleries (ʾatiqim) in three stories over against each other. The three-tiered gallery structure (ʾatiq ʾel-mul ʾatiq shelosh peʿamim) echoes the three-tiered side chambers of ch 41. The repetition of the three-tiered structure in both the sanctuary side chambers and the priestly chambers emphasizes the architectural principle of ascending order.
4: A walkway (mahalakh) in front of the chambers — ten cubits wide and a hundred cubits long: upetiḥam latsafon — their openings facing north. Mahalakh (walkway/passage, from halakh, to walk) is the corridor that gives access to the chambers. The north-facing orientation is the priests' access side — approaching from the outer court rather than from the sanctuary proper.
5: The upper chambers are shorter than the lower and middle ones because the galleries (ʾatiqim) take up more of their space — ki yiʾkhlelu ha-ʾatiqim mimmennah min-hattaḥtonot umin-hattikhonot mibbenavyan. This describes a structural feature of the gallery system: each level's floor space is reduced by the gallery width, so the uppermost rooms are shortest. The engineering precision of this description suggests the vision is not symbolic schematic but architectural reality.
6: The three-story chambers have no pillars (ki eyn lahem ʿammudim kaʿammudei haḥatserot) like the pillars of the courts. The absence of the free-standing columns that support colonnaded courts distinguishes the priestly chambers from public colonnaded spaces. The structural support comes from the walls themselves rather than columns, making the chambers more enclosed and private.
7: The outer wall (gadar) — a single wall of fifty cubits along the outer court in front of the chambers. Gadar (wall built of stones, as distinct from ḥomah, a city/boundary wall) is used for a structural dividing wall. The fifty-cubit wall creates the outer boundary of the priestly chamber precinct relative to the outer court.
8: The length before the outer chambers is fifty cubits — ve-ʿal-penei hallishakhot ʾasher larabbim ʾorek ḥamishim ʾammah — while the nave length is one hundred cubits. The fifty/one hundred ratio of the outer chambers to the full nave maintains the proportional system of the entire complex.
9: Below these chambers — mitaḥat hallishakhot haʾelle — the entrance from the east side as one enters from the outer court. The lower entrance (mevoh lahen miqqedem bevo lahen miḥatser haḥitsonah) connects the outer court to the priestly chamber precinct. The access pattern — entering from the east side at the lower level — parallels YHWH's glory entering from the east (43:1-4).
10: In the wall of the court toward the east — chambers facing south (derom) before the separated yard and before the building. The south-side chambers are the mirror of the north-side chambers, creating the bilateral symmetry of the priestly precinct. The directional description makes explicit the four-sided enclosure of the sanctuary zone.
11: The walkway before them (derekh lifnehem) — like the appearance of the north chambers — same length, same width, same exits and arrangements (kemishpateyhen vekhifteḥeyhen). The exact parallelism between north and south priestly chambers is stated explicitly: kekhol toʾarotam vekhifteḥeyhen ukhematkhnotam (all their forms and all their exits and all their arrangements). The principle of mirror-symmetry in the priestly precinct is architectural theology: the bilateral structure encodes the completeness of priestly service.
12: The entrance to the south chambers — lifnei hagadar hadarek ʾasher qedem bevoʾam — "before the wall, the way as one enters from the east." The access pattern from the east is repeated for the south chambers as for the north. The east-entry convention for both priestly chamber complexes connects to the east-facing orientation of the entire sanctuary.
13: lishkhot hatstasfon velishtot hadarom ʾasher lifnei haggizrah — the north and south chambers before the separated yard are the holy chambers (lishkhot haqqodesh) where priests who approach YHWH eat the most holy offerings (qodshei haqqodashim — a double plural of the superlative, "the holiest holy things"). The specific activities for these chambers: eating the grain offering (minḥah), sin offering (ḥattat), and guilt offering (ʾasham). The three sacrifice types that require eating in the holy precinct are precisely the three types eaten only within the sanctuary per Lev 6-7.
14: uvevaʾam hakohanim veloʾ yetsʾu min-haqqodesh ʾel-heḥatser haḥitsonah — when the priests enter the holy precinct they shall not go out from the holy place to the outer court without leaving there their vestments in which they minister. The consecration principle: priestly garments yeniḥu vigdeyhem ʾasher yesharteru vahem (they shall lay aside the garments in which they serve) before entering the outer court, because these garments are holy (qodesh hem) and must not carry holiness into the common space. The levushot aḥerot (other garments) worn in the outer court are the boundary between sacred and common space made textile.
15: The outer court measurement upon completion — ukhilah ʾet-midot habbayit hapenimi (having finished measuring the inner temple) — the guide takes Ezekiel out through the east gate to measure the outer boundary. The east gate as exit parallels the east gate as entry: the east is both the entrance and the exit of the sanctuary vision, framing the entire measurement sequence.
16: The east side: five hundred reeds by the measuring rod — ḥamesh meʾot ḥamesh meʾot qane hamiddah. The measurement is by the full measuring rod (six long cubits each rod, per 40:5), not by individual cubits. The outer boundary at five hundred reeds × four sides creates the enormous perimeter of the holy district.
17: The north side: five hundred reeds by the measuring rod. The bilateral symmetry of north and south, east and west at the outer boundary confirms the perfect square of the entire holy precinct at the macro-scale.
18: The south side: five hundred reeds by the measuring rod. The systematic measurement of all four sides in sequence — east, north, south, west (reversing to west in v19) — follows the orientation convention that places east first (the face/front of the sanctuary).
19: The west side: five hundred reeds by the measuring rod. The four-directional completion of the outer boundary measurement corresponds to the four-directional completeness of the sanctuary's holiness. The five-hundred-reed measurement on each side creates a perfect square of 500 × 500 reeds.
20: lehavchal ven-haqqodesh uven-haḥol — the wall had a measurement of five hundred cubits on each side to make a separation between the holy and the common: lehavchal ven-haqqodesh uven-haḥol. The double terms qodesh (holy/sacred) and ḥol (common/profane) express the fundamental binary of the Israelite cult. Lehavchal (to divide/separate, hiphil of badal, the same root as mavdil, divider) — the wall's ultimate purpose is to embody the distinction that is foundational to Israel's entire theological and ritual system. This ḥomah (boundary wall) is YHWH's architectural encoding of the holy/common distinction that governs all of life.
